title: Caching Interview
description: Senior-level caching interview quiz covering cache patterns, consistency, invalidation, and operational tradeoffs

questions.question: Why is cache invalidation considered one of the hardest distributed systems problems?
options: Data changes and access patterns are dynamic, making freshness and performance tradeoffs context-dependent
Invalidation APIs are missing in modern cache products
questions.explanation: Invalidation must balance staleness risk, update frequency, and latency/cost goals. There is no universal policy that fits all workloads.

questions.question: What is the main difference between cache-aside and read-through caching?
options: Cache-aside pushes cache population to application logic; read-through delegates loading to cache layer
questions.explanation: In cache-aside, the app handles misses and writes to cache. In read-through, the cache provider handles loading from the backing store.

questions.question: When is write-through caching usually beneficial?
options: When write latency must be minimized above all else
When cache and store should remain closely aligned on writes at the expense of write path overhead
questions.explanation: Write-through updates cache and source of truth together, improving read freshness consistency but adding write latency and complexity.

questions.question: What is a key risk of write-back (write-behind) caches?
options: Higher durability risk if cache data is lost before backend flush
questions.explanation: Write-back improves write throughput/latency but introduces potential durability gaps unless carefully designed with persistence and replay.

questions.question: What problem does request coalescing solve in hot-key scenarios?
options: It deduplicates concurrent misses so only one backend fetch occurs
questions.explanation: Coalescing (single-flight) prevents duplicate expensive backend calls when many requests miss the same key simultaneously.

questions.question: Cache stampede is best described as:
options: Many clients concurrently recomputing/refetching expired data causing backend overload
A cache node running out of memory
questions.explanation: Stampedes occur when a popular key expires and many requests miss at once, often overloading origin services.

questions.question: Which strategy most directly mitigates cache stampedes?
options: Jittered TTLs, request coalescing, and stale-while-revalidate
questions.explanation: Combining staggered expirations and controlled regeneration reduces thundering-herd effects and origin spikes.

questions.question: What is stale-while-revalidate primarily optimizing for?
options: Low read latency by serving stale data briefly while refreshing in background
questions.explanation: This pattern accepts bounded staleness to protect latency and availability during refresh.

questions.question: Why is TTL-only invalidation often insufficient for critical correctness paths?
options: TTL values are always too short
Data may remain stale until expiry unless proactive invalidation/versioning is used
TTL cannot be configured per key
questions.explanation: TTL alone gives eventual freshness, not immediate consistency. Important flows often require event-driven invalidation or version checks.

questions.question: What is the role of cache key versioning (e.g., user:42:v17)?
options: Support safe invalidation by changing key namespace when data version changes
questions.explanation: Versioned keys let old entries age out naturally while new reads target fresh keys, reducing delete-race complexity.

questions.question: In multi-layer caching (CDN, edge, app, DB), what is a frequent pitfall?
options: Inconsistent invalidation semantics and TTLs across layers causing hard-to-debug staleness
questions.explanation: Layered caches are powerful but require coordinated invalidation and telemetry; otherwise stale data behavior becomes unpredictable.

questions.question: What metric best indicates cache effectiveness for backend protection?
options: Origin offload plus miss latency and miss volume by key cohort
questions.explanation: Hit rate can be misleading. You need miss behavior and origin offload metrics to understand real capacity impact.

questions.question: Which eviction policy is generally best for skewed workloads with recency locality?
options: LRU or variants approximating recency-aware behavior
questions.explanation: Recency-aware policies often perform well when recently accessed items are likely to be reused soon.

questions.question: What tradeoff does LFU-style eviction make relative to pure LRU?
options: LFU tracks frequency, helping retain long-term hot items but can be less responsive to rapid trend shifts
LFU guarantees higher hit ratio in all workloads
questions.explanation: LFU is strong for persistent hot keys; LRU adapts faster to changing access recency. Hybrid policies often work best in practice.

questions.question: Why can very large cached objects reduce overall cache efficiency?
options: They increase hit ratio by default
They consume disproportionate memory, causing churn and eviction of many smaller hot objects
questions.explanation: Object size distribution matters; a few oversized entries can degrade cache utility for broader request patterns.

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of consistent hashing in distributed cache clusters?
options: Minimize key remapping when nodes are added or removed
questions.explanation: Consistent hashing improves rebalance stability and reduces mass cache invalidation during topology changes.

questions.question: Why should cache entries for permission/auth data be treated with stricter freshness controls?
options: Stale auth decisions can create security vulnerabilities
questions.explanation: Authorization correctness has high security impact. Invalidations and bounded staleness windows must align with risk tolerance.

questions.question: What is negative caching?
options: Caching failed lookups or not-found responses briefly to reduce repeated backend work
Removing all missing keys from cache
questions.explanation: Negative caching can protect origins from repeated misses on absent resources, but TTLs should be conservative.

questions.question: What is a common danger of overly long negative-cache TTLs?
options: Serving stale not-found responses after data is created
questions.explanation: If missing-state is cached too long, newly created resources may appear absent until expiry.

questions.question: When using Redis as a cache, what is a production risk of mixing cache and durable queue/state workloads in one cluster?
options: Resource contention and eviction behavior can affect critical non-cache data
questions.explanation: Shared clusters can cause noisy-neighbor effects. Critical durable data should be isolated from volatile cache pressure.

questions.question: What is cache warming and when is it useful?
options: Pre-populating likely hot keys before traffic surges or deployments
Replacing lazy loading in all cases
questions.explanation: Warming reduces cold-start miss spikes, especially after restarts or known traffic events.

questions.question: Which scenario most justifies bypassing cache for a read request?
options: Consistency-critical operation requiring freshest state (e.g., post-payment confirmation)
questions.explanation: Some business flows need strong freshness and should read directly from source-of-truth or use version checks.

questions.question: Why are cache tags or group invalidation keys valuable?
options: They allow invalidating related sets of entries without enumerating every key
questions.explanation: Group invalidation simplifies consistency management for derived content linked to shared entities.

questions.question: What is a key challenge with event-driven cache invalidation?
options: Missed, delayed, or out-of-order events can leave stale cache state
It cannot work with message brokers
questions.explanation: Event pipelines require idempotency, ordering strategy, and recovery mechanisms to keep cache state reliable.

questions.question: How does write-through plus read-through differ operationally from cache-aside?
options: It centralizes cache logic but can reduce application flexibility
questions.explanation: Managed cache patterns can reduce app complexity but may constrain custom behavior and require deeper cache infrastructure trust.

questions.question: What is the most likely effect of setting identical TTLs for all high-traffic keys?
options: Synchronized expirations causing bursty origin load
questions.explanation: Uniform expirations can align misses in time. TTL jitter spreads refresh load and reduces spikes.

questions.question: In edge/CDN caching, why is cache-key normalization important?
options: It reduces key cardinality explosion from irrelevant query/header variations
questions.explanation: Unnormalized keys fragment cache entries and reduce hit rates. Key design should include only variation dimensions that affect response content.

questions.question: What does a high cache hit ratio with high user latency often suggest?
options: Bottlenecks may exist elsewhere (network, app processing, downstream calls)
questions.explanation: Cache metrics are one piece of performance. End-to-end latency can still be dominated by non-cache components.

questions.question: Why should cache invalidation paths be part of incident playbooks?
options: Emergency purge and selective invalidation are common recovery actions for bad deploys/data
questions.explanation: Operational readiness includes safe invalidation procedures to recover quickly from corrupted or stale cached content.

questions.question: What is the purpose of soft TTL and hard TTL together?
options: Soft TTL allows stale serving during refresh window; hard TTL caps maximum staleness
Hard TTL is for writes only
questions.explanation: Two-tier expiry provides resilience under load while still enforcing absolute freshness boundaries.

questions.question: Which caching choice is best for highly personalized data with strict privacy boundaries?
options: Global shared cache key per endpoint
Per-user scoped keys with careful authorization-aware variation
questions.explanation: Personalized content must isolate cache entries by identity and relevant auth context to prevent data leaks.

questions.question: What is the strongest reason to include cache-related telemetry dimensions by key family?
options: Different key cohorts have different hit/miss/staleness behavior and business impact
questions.explanation: Per-key-family observability reveals where cache strategy works or fails, enabling targeted tuning.

questions.question: Why might cache-aside writes produce stale reads temporarily?
options: Because writes to source and cache are not atomic unless explicitly coordinated
Because caches cannot be updated after writes
questions.explanation: Typical write flow updates DB then invalidates cache, leaving a brief window for races unless protected by ordering/idempotent logic.

questions.question: When does local in-process caching become risky?
options: When many app instances serve mutable data and coherence requirements are strict
questions.explanation: Per-instance caches can diverge. Distributed invalidation or centralized cache layers may be required for consistency-sensitive data.

questions.question: What does cache poisoning refer to in API/platform security?
options: Malicious or incorrect content being cached and served broadly
questions.explanation: If untrusted input influences cache keys/variants incorrectly, attackers can inject or misdirect cached responses.

questions.question: Which control helps reduce cache poisoning risk in HTTP caching layers?
options: Strict cache key construction and validated Vary/header handling
questions.explanation: Security-aware key/variant rules and header sanitization prevent untrusted dimensions from polluting shared cache entries.

questions.question: Why is benchmarking cache changes with production-like key distributions important?
options: Synthetic uniform traffic can hide hotspot and eviction behavior seen in real workloads
Cache hit ratio is independent of distribution
questions.explanation: Real access skew shapes hit rates, memory pressure, and origin load. Representative distributions are necessary for reliable decisions.

questions.question: What is a common false assumption when scaling cache clusters horizontally?
options: Adding nodes always improves hit ratio without rebalancing impact
Node changes can temporarily lower hit ratio due to key movement
questions.explanation: Scaling out may cause key redistribution and temporary coldness. Prewarming and controlled rollout mitigate this effect.

questions.question: For cache outages, what fallback pattern is most resilient?
options: Graceful degradation with bounded origin protection and circuit breakers
questions.explanation: When cache is unavailable, systems should degrade predictably, protect origins, and avoid cascading failures.

questions.question: What is the strongest sign of mature caching architecture?
options: Clear consistency model, measured business impact, safe invalidation, and tested failure playbooks
questions.explanation: Mature caching is operational and product-aware: correctness, performance, observability, and resilience are all engineered intentionally.
